stimulus: A team of plant biologists led by Hana Okabe, Diego Ramirez, and Léa Fontaine studied the mechanism by which Lemna minor (common duckweed) accelerates its rate of vegetative reproduction under increased levels of dissolved nitrogen. They replaced the regulatory protein NRX2 in the duckweed with a structurally similar protein from a closely related species, Spirodela polyrhiza, which does not show accelerated reproduction at elevated nitrogen levels. At low nitrogen concentrations, unmodified and modified L. minor plants reproduced at the same rate, but at high nitrogen concentrations, the unmodified plants reproduced markedly faster while the modified plants did not, which suggests that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: nitrogen-sensitive accelerated reproduction is unique to L. minor among all aquatic plants.
NRX2 enables L. minor to respond to increased levels of dissolved nitrogen.
L. minor produces more NRX2 protein as dissolved nitrogen levels rise.
certain minimum levels of dissolved nitrogen are required for L. minor to reproduce at all.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The only experimental variable changed was the identity of the regulatory protein (NRX2 versus the version from S. polyrhiza). Plants with native NRX2 sped up reproduction at high nitrogen; plants in which NRX2 was replaced did not. The most direct inference is that NRX2 is what allows L. minor to respond to elevated nitrogen.

Choice A is incorrect because the experiment compared only two species; it does not justify a claim about all aquatic plants. Choice C is incorrect because the study did not measure the amount of NRX2 produced — only the effects of swapping it out. Choice D is incorrect because both plant groups reproduced normally at low nitrogen, so nitrogen is not shown to be required for reproduction.
